title: Email Inbox & Sending Domains
lede: Turn iMash into your team's email command center: send branded email from your own domain, receive replies in a shared Mailbox, connect existing Gmail/Outlook accounts, and let AI draft your replies. This guide walks you A-to-Z through every tab of Messages → Email.

steps.title: Open Messages → Email and meet the four tabs
steps.content:
Everything email lives on one page — your inbox, your domain setup, your campaigns, and your access controls.

  1. In the top navigation, click Messages, then click the Email sub-tab (or use Email in the left sidebar under Messages).
  2. The page opens on the Mailbox tab. Across the top you'll see four tabs:
  • Mailbox — the shared inbox: read, reply, forward, and compose email for all your connected addresses.
  • Sending Domains — verify a domain you own so iMash can send branded email as you@yourcompany.com, plus connect existing Gmail / Outlook / IMAP mailboxes.
  • Marketing — bulk email campaigns with open/click tracking (covered in its own guide — see What's Next below).
  • Settings — sending warm-up schedules and sub-account permissions (main account only).
💡 The tab you're on is saved in the page URL, so you can bookmark or share a link straight to any tab.

images.alt: Messages Email page open on the Mailbox tab showing the thread list, reader pane and the four tabs
images.caption: Messages → Email — Mailbox, Sending Domains, Marketing, and Settings tabs on one page

images.alt: Messages Email page open on the Mailbox tab showing the thread list, reader pane and the four tabs
images.caption: Messages → Email — Mailbox, Sending Domains, Marketing, and Settings tabs on one page

steps.title: Add your sending domain
steps.content:
This is the step that makes your email look professional: mail goes out from your domain with full DKIM authentication — never from a shared iMash address.

  1. Click the Sending Domains tab.
  2. In the Add a sending domain card, type a domain you own (for example yourcompany.com).
  3. Click Add & get DNS records.

A card appears for your domain with a Pending status tag and a table of DNS records to publish (next step).

💡 You can paste a full URL or an email address — iMash extracts just the domain. You can add as many domains as you like, and each one gets its own card with its own addresses.

images.alt: Sending Domains tab with the Add a sending domain card and the Connected mailboxes section
images.caption: The Sending Domains tab — add your own domain at the top; connected mailboxes live below

images.alt: Sending Domains tab with the Add a sending domain card and the Connected mailboxes section
images.caption: The Sending Domains tab — add your own domain at the top; connected mailboxes live below

images.alt: Add a sending domain card with the domain input and Add and get DNS records button
images.caption: Type your domain and click Add & get DNS records

images.alt: Add a sending domain card with the domain input and Add and get DNS records button
images.caption: Type your domain and click Add & get DNS records

steps.title: Publish the DNS records and verify
steps.content:
Your domain card shows a DNS-records table. Each row has Type, Host / Name, Value, and Priority columns, with a copy button next to every name and value. The rows are grouped:

  • DKIM (3 CNAME records, tagged blue — required) — these prove the mail really comes from you. Sending unlocks once they verify.
  • MAIL FROM (MX + SPF TXT — optional) — aligns the bounce address with your domain for better deliverability.
  • DMARC (TXT — optional, advisory) — tells receiving servers how to treat unauthenticated mail claiming to be you.
  • MX (receiving) (optional) — publish this only if you also want to receive mail in iMash (see the addresses step).
  1. In your DNS provider (GoDaddy, Cloudflare, Namecheap…), create each record exactly as shown — copy/paste the Host / Name and Value using the copy icons.
  2. Back in iMash, click Check verification in the domain card's top-right corner.
  3. Watch the per-record indicators: a green check means the record was found in DNS; a clock means it hasn't propagated yet.

Once verified, the status tag flips to Verified, the records tuck away into a collapsible DNS records — verified, shown for reference section, and the DKIM / MAIL FROM status pills turn green.

⚠️ DNS changes usually propagate in minutes but can take a few hours — and after the records are visible, verification itself can take a little longer (worst case up to 72 hours). If Check verification says all records were detected, you're done: it completes automatically, no action needed.

steps.title: Create addresses and set your sender identity
steps.content:
A verified domain unlocks two extra sections inside its card:

Addresses — the mailboxes on your domain:

  1. Type a name in the box (e.g. contact) — the field shows the full result, like contact@yourcompany.com.
  2. Click Add address. The address can send immediately.
  3. Click Name & signature next to any address to set its Sender name (shown to recipients as Name <you@domain>), a Signature appended to every email sent from the address — manual and AI replies alike — and an optional Signature logo image.
  4. To also receive mail at your addresses, publish the MX (receiving) record from the DNS table and click Check verification — the card then shows an MX verified pill and each address lists where it receives (for example name@inbox.yourcompany.com in subdomain mode).

Tracking domain (optional but recommended) — brands the open/click/unsubscribe links in marketing email with your own domain:

  1. Keep the suggested track.yourcompany.com (or type your own subdomain) and click Save.
  2. Publish the single CNAME record shown, then click Check tracking DNS.
  3. When the tag reads Verified, your campaign links (and your signature logo) are served from your own domain — which looks legitimate to spam filters.
💡 Receiving defaults to a safe subdomain mode (inbox.yourdomain.com) that never interferes with your existing company mail. Full-domain receiving is possible too, but it takes over ALL mail for the domain — only use it for domains dedicated to iMash.

steps.title: Or connect an existing Gmail / Outlook / IMAP mailbox
steps.content:
Already living in Gmail or Outlook? Scroll below the domain cards on the Sending Domains tab to the Connected mailboxes section — it plugs your existing account straight into the iMash Mailbox.

Each connected mailbox row shows the address, a provider tag (GMAIL / OUTLOOK / IMAP), a connection status, the SMTP/IMAP hosts, and the sync mode. The row actions are:

  • Sync now — pull new mail immediately instead of waiting for the automatic background sync (every few minutes).
  • Edit — change the connection settings, sender name, or signature.
  • Resume sync — appears when sync was auto-paused after repeated failures (bad password, missing IMAP host). Fix the settings and save to resume.
  • Disconnect (broken-link icon) — stops syncing but keeps the email history; a Re-enable link brings it back.
  • Delete (trash icon) — permanently removes the mailbox and all emails linked to it. This cannot be undone.

images.alt: A connected mailbox row with provider tag, sync status and the Sync now, Edit, disconnect and delete actions
images.caption: A connected mailbox row — Sync now, Edit, Resume sync, disconnect, and delete

images.alt: A connected mailbox row with provider tag, sync status and the Sync now, Edit, disconnect and delete actions
images.caption: A connected mailbox row — Sync now, Edit, Resume sync, disconnect, and delete

steps.title: Add a mailbox with an app password
steps.content:
1. Click Add mailbox in the top-right of the Mailboxes card. The Add mailbox (App Password / SMTP) window opens.
  2. Pick your Provider: Gmail, Outlook, or IMAP/Custom.
  3. Enter the From Email Address — the address you're connecting.
  4. Fill in the SMTP Host and SMTP Port (for Gmail: smtp.gmail.com port 465). These are required for sending.
  5. Optionally add the IMAP Host and IMAP Port (Gmail: imap.gmail.com port 993) so iMash can also receive and sync your inbox.
  6. Skip older emails — leave it off to import your existing inbox in the background and then auto-sync; turn it on to only sync mail that arrives from now on.
  7. Enter your Username (usually the email address) and App Password.
  8. Under Sender identity, set the Sender name and Signature; the Signature logo can be added after the first save (reopen the mailbox with Edit).
  9. Click Save — the initial import starts immediately in the background.
⚠️ Gmail and Outlook require an App Password, not your normal login password. In Gmail, enable 2-factor authentication first, then create one under Google Account → Security → App passwords.

images.alt: Add mailbox modal with provider, SMTP and IMAP fields, skip older emails toggle, username, app password and sender identity
images.caption: The Add mailbox window — SMTP sends, IMAP receives, and the sender identity signs your mail

images.alt: Add mailbox modal with provider, SMTP and IMAP fields, skip older emails toggle, username, app password and sender identity
images.caption: The Add mailbox window — SMTP sends, IMAP receives, and the sender identity signs your mail

steps.title: Read your mail in the Mailbox
steps.content:
Back on the Mailbox tab, all your addresses flow into one inbox — SES domain addresses and connected Gmail/Outlook/IMAP mailboxes together, grouped into conversation threads.

  • Thread list (left) — the newest conversations first. A filled blue dot marks an unread thread; click the dot to toggle read/unread without opening it. A badge next to the Mailbox title counts unread threads.
  • Refresh (circular arrow at the top of the list) — pulls new mail from your providers right now.
  • Reader (right) — click a thread to read every message in it, including rich HTML bodies and images. Attachments appear as chips you can click to open or download.
  • Filters (top-left button) — opens a filter bar with: a date range preset (All time, Today, Yesterday, This week, This month, Custom), a Direction selector (All / Inbox / Sent), a Show delivery failures checkbox (bounce notifications are hidden by default), a Mailboxes picker to focus on specific addresses, three search boxes (Search email addresses, Search subjects, Search message content), and Accounts / Contacts / Leads pickers to see only mail linked to a CRM record. Click Apply Filters to run them, or Clear All to reset.
💡 The same Mailbox also appears inside each contact's and lead's detail drawer, pre-filtered to that person — perfect for checking history mid-call.

images.alt: Mailbox filter bar with date preset, direction, delivery failures checkbox, mailbox picker, searches and CRM record filters
images.caption: The Filters bar — date, direction, mailbox, text search, and CRM-record filters

images.alt: Mailbox filter bar with date preset, direction, delivery failures checkbox, mailbox picker, searches and CRM record filters
images.caption: The Filters bar — date, direction, mailbox, text search, and CRM-record filters

steps.title: Reply — or let AI write it for you
steps.content:
Every message in the reader has an action row at the bottom:

  • Reply — answer the sender. Reply All appears when the thread has multiple recipients.
  • Forward — send the message to someone new.
  • Reply with AI — the star of the show: opens the reply composer and immediately drafts a complete, context-aware reply from the whole conversation. Review, tweak, send.
  • The gear icon next to it opens the AI writer settings, where you set the tone and standing instructions the AI should always follow.

The inline composer that opens gives you:

  1. From — which of your mailboxes sends the reply.
  2. To, plus Show CC/BCC for extra recipients — start typing to search your contacts and leads by name.
  3. Subject — pre-filled with Re: the original subject.
  4. Body — write it yourself, click Generate with AI to draft from scratch, or select Rewrite to have AI polish what you wrote (make it shorter, friendlier, add a call to action…). Settings opens the same AI writer preferences.
  5. Attachments — click or drag files into the drop zone.
  6. Click Send.
💡 Replies keep proper email threading, so the conversation stays intact in your customer's inbox too — no broken "new email" chains.

images.alt: Inline reply composer with From, To, Subject, AI draft actions, attachments drop zone and Send button
images.caption: The inline reply composer — Generate with AI, Rewrite, attachments, and Send

images.alt: Inline reply composer with From, To, Subject, AI draft actions, attachments drop zone and Send button
images.caption: The inline reply composer — Generate with AI, Rewrite, attachments, and Send

steps.title: Compose a brand-new email
steps.content:
1. Click New Email in the top-right of the Mailbox tab.
  2. Pick the sending mailbox in the From selector — every address you're allowed to send from is listed.
  3. Add recipients in To (type to search contacts and leads, or paste any address). Click Show CC/BCC for copy recipients.
  4. Enter a Subject.
  5. Write your message in the Body — or click Generate with AI to draft it and Rewrite to refine it. Toggle HTML editor on if you want to paste rich HTML.
  6. Drag files into Attachments if needed.
  7. Click Send.
💡 Sent mail lands in the same thread list — switch the Direction filter to Sent to review everything that went out.

images.alt: New Email window with From selector, To field, CC BCC link, subject, AI-assisted body and attachments
images.caption: New Email — pick the From mailbox, add recipients, and draft with AI

images.alt: New Email window with From selector, To field, CC BCC link, subject, AI-assisted body and attachments
images.caption: New Email — pick the From mailbox, add recipients, and draft with AI

steps.title: Protect deliverability with sending warm-up (Settings tab)
steps.content:
The Settings tab (visible to the main account) is where you protect your sender reputation. New domains that suddenly blast thousands of emails get flagged — warm-up prevents that.

  1. Click the Settings tab.
  2. In the Sending warm-up card, each verified domain gets its own row with a toggle.
  3. Turn the toggle on to start warming a domain. Pick a Schedule preset (hover the summary to see the exact day-by-day caps) and confirm the Started date — day 1 of the ramp.
  4. The orange tag on the row shows where you are: Day N · cap/day. The cap rises automatically each day.

Warm-up only limits marketing sends — transactional email and AI replies are never delayed. Above the domain rows, an info banner shows your account daily marketing cap, a hard ceiling across all your domains set by your platform admin.

images.alt: Email Settings tab with the Sending warm-up card and sub-account permissions table
images.caption: The Settings tab — warm-up, account cap, and sub-account permissions

images.alt: Email Settings tab with the Sending warm-up card and sub-account permissions table
images.caption: The Settings tab — warm-up, account cap, and sub-account permissions

images.alt: Sending warm-up card explaining the per-domain marketing ramp and account daily cap
images.caption: Sending warm-up — a per-domain daily ramp that protects your reputation

images.alt: Sending warm-up card explaining the per-domain marketing ramp and account daily cap
images.caption: Sending warm-up — a per-domain daily ramp that protects your reputation

steps.title: Control what your team can send from
steps.content:
Below warm-up, three cards give the main account full control over sub-account email access:

  • Sub-account permissions — a table of your sub-accounts with a Can add sending domains switch per row. Turn it on to let that team member verify their own domains (still under your account's sending tenant).
  • Domain access — for each domain, choose who may send from it: All sub-accounts, Only me, or Selected (pick specific sub-accounts from the dropdown that appears).
  • Address access — the same three-way choice per individual mailbox, covering both your domain addresses and connected Gmail / Outlook / IMAP mailboxes. Whoever added a domain or connected a mailbox can always use it.
💡 New domains a sub-account adds default to All sub-accounts access — tighten it here if a domain should stay private.

images.alt: Address access card with the All sub-accounts, Only me and Selected options for one mailbox
images.caption: Per-mailbox access — All sub-accounts, Only me, or Selected

images.alt: Address access card with the All sub-accounts, Only me and Selected options for one mailbox
images.caption: Per-mailbox access — All sub-accounts, Only me, or Selected

support.title: Need Help?
support.content: Stuck on DNS records or a mailbox that won't sync? Email us at support@imash.io — include your domain name and a screenshot of the domain card, and we'll get you verified.

metaTitle: Email Inbox & Sending Domains | iMash Documentation
metaDescription: Set up branded email in iMash: verify a sending domain with DKIM DNS records, create addresses, connect Gmail/Outlook/IMAP mailboxes, read and reply with AI in the shared Mailbox, and manage warm-up and sub-account access.
